attributes.stem:
A 17-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a four-week history of daily fevers above 39°C, joint pain, weight loss, wet cough and a salmon-pink rash that appears during fever episodes. The fevers spike consistently in the morning and evening. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He is a high school student and travelled to Vietnam six weeks previously.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 39.5°C. There is a non-pruritic, salmon-pink macular rash on his trunk and proximal limbs. Several joints including his wrists, knees, and ankles are tender and warm. Cardiorespiratory examination reveals a left pleural effusion.

Investigations reveal: haemoglobin 105 g/L (115-165), white cell count 15.5 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0), platelets 450 × 10⁹/L (150-400), C-reactive protein 220 mg/L (<5), ferritin 5000 μg/L (20-200). Blood cultures, antinuclear antibodies, and rheumatoid factor are negative. Thick and thin blood smear is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of Adult-onset Still's disease: high spiking fevers, salmon-pink evanescent rash, and arthralgia/arthritis. The markedly elevated ferritin (typically five times the normal upper limit), negative autoantibodies, and characteristic rash support the diagnosis. Adult-onset Still's disease is a rare immune-mediated inflammatory disorder that typically affects young adults between 16-35 years. The diagnosis is based on clinical criteria, as there is no definitive diagnostic test. The Yamaguchi criteria are most commonly used, requiring the presence of major criteria (fever, arthralgia, typical rash, leucocytosis) and minor criteria (sore throat, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, abnormal liver function tests, negative RF and ANA) after exclusion of other conditions.

Prednisolone is the most appropriate initial treatment for moderate to severe Adult-onset Still's disease. Glucocorticoids effectively suppress the systemic inflammation and provide rapid symptom relief. Anti-inflammatories may be used for minor flares.

Benzylpenicillin would not be appropriate as this is not an infectious condition. While the presentation might initially suggest infection, the characteristic rash, pattern of symptoms, relatively low white cell count and negative blood cultures point away from a bacterial cause.

Methotrexate can be used as a steroid-sparing agent in Adult-onset Still's disease, but it is not the first-line treatment. It is typically added later in the disease course if steroid dependence develops or in cases of persistent arthritis.

Anakinra, an interleukin-1 receptor antagonist, is highly effective in Adult-onset Still's disease but is usually reserved for refractory cases or as a steroid-sparing agent. It is not typically used as first-line therapy due to cost and the need to establish the diagnosis definitively.

Artemisinin-based combination therapy would be appropriate for treating malaria. While the patient has recently travelled to Vietnam, the characteristic rash, joint involvement, and extremely high ferritin are not consistent with malaria and point more towards Adult-onset Still's disease.
learningPoints: Adult-onset Still's disease classically presents with high spiking fevers, salmon-pink evanescent rash, and arthralgia/arthritis.
Glucocorticoids are first-line therapy for moderate to severe Adult-onset Still's disease.
Markedly elevated ferritin (often >5 times upper limit) with negative autoantibodies supports the diagnosis of Adult-onset Still's disease.
references: Leavis H et al. Management of adult-onset Still's disease: evidence- and consensus-based recommendations by experts, Rheumatology. 2024: 63 (6).  Available at: https://academic.oup.com/rheumatology/article/63/6/1656/7260500
Tomaras S, Goetzke CC, Kallinich T, Feist E. Adult-Onset Still's Disease: Clinical Aspects and Therapeutic Approach. J Clin Med. 2021.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7918550/
